analysis.note: Genitive plural depending on paramārthaḥ: “the paramārtha of dharmas.” The commentary narrows these as sarvadharmāṇām paratantrātmakānām (trbh 1418–1419).
Predicate nominative with saḥ; its technical sense is fixed by Sthiramati as the object/referent of supreme supramundane cognition and as the pariniṣpanna-svabhāva.

verification.detail: compound stem paramārtha as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Anaphoric pronoun: Sthiramati identifies it with pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ.
Causal relative adverb, “since/because”; Sthiramati paraphrases it with yasmāt ... tasmāt.
Predicate nominative: “suchness.” In sandhi with api, the cluster is printed tathatāpi.
The commentary explicitly comments on apiśabda: it does not merely add one name, but indicates that all dharmadhātu-synonyms are applicable.
Again masculine because it refers to pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ, not to the feminine tathatā.
Lexicalized adverbial accusative: “at all times, always.” The commentary repeats sarvakālam with bhavati.
Ablative of cause: “because of being so.” It is tathā-bhāva, not tathā-abhāva; Sthiramati glosses with tathaiva bhavati nānyathā.

verification.detail: compound stem tathābhāva as Pum/Panchami/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Feminine nominative agreeing with tathatā/vijñaptimātratā. In sandhi with eva, the cluster is printed saiva.
Restrictive/emphatic: “that very.” In sandhi with sā, the cluster is printed saiva.
A single feminine abstract noun in -tā, “representation-only-ness.” The inner base is vijñapti-mātra; the final -tā is not a separate verse-word.

verification.detail: compound stem vijñaptimātratā as Stri/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: Do not translate paramārthaḥ merely as an ordinary “highest purpose/meaning.” Sthiramati gives a technical nirukti and then identifies it with pariniṣpanna-svabhāva, specifically as the paramārtha of all dharmas whose nature is dependent.
options_considered: generic “ultimate truth” without specifying its cognitive/referential sense
commentarially defined ultimate object/reality, the pariniṣpanna-svabhāva
justifications.chosen: paramārthaḥ means the ultimate object/reality: the perfected nature as the object of supreme supramundane cognition and as the ultimate of dependent-nature dharmas.

evidence.quote: paramaṃ hi lokottarajñānaṃ niruttaratvāt tasyārthaḥ paramārthaḥ
evidence.translation: For supreme supramundane cognition, because it is unsurpassed, is ‘parama’; its object/referent is ‘paramārtha’.

evidence.quote:
atha
vākāśavat sarvatraikarasārthena vaimalyāvikārārthena ca pariniṣpannaḥ
svabhāvaḥ paramārtha ucyate
evidence.translation: Or, the perfected nature is called paramārtha in the sense that it is uniform everywhere like space, and in the sense of purity and non-alteration.

evidence.quote:
sa yasmāt pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ sarvadharmāṇāṃ
paratantrātmakānāṃ paramārthaḥ
taddharmateti kṛtvā
evidence.translation: Because that perfected nature is the paramārtha of all dharmas, which have dependent nature, being their dharmatā.

justifications.decision: The verse’s sa yatas is to be construed through the commentary’s sa yasmāt ... tasmāt, not as a spatial “whence.” The masculine pronoun is not referring to tathatā but to the perfected nature.
options_considered: saḥ as referring to the feminine tathatā
causal yatas, with saḥ anaphoric to pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ
justifications.chosen: yatas is causal, “since/because,” and saḥ refers to pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ.

evidence.quote:
sa yasmāt pariniṣpannaḥ svabhāvaḥ sarvadharmāṇāṃ
paratantrātmakānāṃ paramārthaḥ
taddharmateti kṛtvā tasmāt pariniṣpanna eva
svabhāvaḥ paramārthaniḥsvabhāvātā pariniṣpannasyābhāvasvabhāvatvāt
evidence.translation: Because that perfected nature is the paramārtha of all dependent-nature dharmas, being their dharmatā, therefore the perfected nature itself is ultimate naturelessness, since the perfected has absence as its nature.

justifications.decision: Translate tathatā as the technical term “suchness,” but do not make api concessive (“although”) or limit the point to one additional synonym. Sthiramati says api indicates that all dharmadhātu-synonyms may designate it.
options_considered: api as indicating inclusion among all dharmadhātu-synonyms
justifications.chosen: tathatā api means “also suchness,” with api marking that this is not the only applicable designation.

evidence.quote:
tathatāpi saḥ
apiśabdān na kevalaṃ tathatāśabdenaivābhidhātavyaḥ
kiṃ tarhi yāvanto
dharmadhātuparyāyāḥ sarvais tair apy abhidhātavya iti
evidence.translation: ‘It is also suchness.’ From the word api: it is not to be designated only by the word suchness; rather, by as many synonyms of dharmadhātu as there are, by all those too it is to be designated.

justifications.decision: The compound is tathā-bhāva, “being so,” in the ablative of cause; it is not tathā-abhāva, “non-being so.” This line explains the name tathatā.
options_considered: a vague “because of such a state”
commentarial “because it is always just so, not otherwise”
justifications.chosen: sarvakālaṃ tathābhāvāt means “because at all times it is just so.”

evidence.quote:
sarvakālaṃ tathābhāvāt
tathatā
tathā hi pṛthagjanaśaikṣāśaikṣāvasthāsu sarvakālaṃ tathaiva bhavati
nānyatheti tathatety ucyate
evidence.translation: ‘Because of being so at all times—suchness.’ For in the states of ordinary person, trainee, and adept, at all times it is just so, not otherwise; therefore it is called suchness.

justifications.decision: Do not take vijñaptimātratā as a different entity or merely as the earlier ordinary thesis of cognition-only. Sthiramati explicitly poses the alternative—whether it is the perfected itself like tathatā, or something else—and answers with sā eva; he adds that this statement teaches realization (abhisamaya).
options_considered: vijñaptimātratā as only an epistemic slogan
identity: that very suchness/perfected nature is vijñaptimātratā, with abhisamaya intended
justifications.chosen: sā eva vijñaptimātratā identifies that very suchness/perfected nature with representation-only-ness.

evidence.quote:
kiṃ punas tathatāvat pariniṣpanna eva vijñaptimātratā
utānyā vijñaptimātratā
ata āha
saiva vijñaptimātratā
evidence.translation: Is vijñaptimātratā, like tathatā, just the perfected, or is vijñaptimātratā something else? Therefore he says: ‘that very is vijñaptimātratā.’
By the statement ‘that very is vijñaptimātratā,’ realization has been taught.

translation:
Since that [perfected nature (pariniṣpanna-svabhāva)] is the ultimate reality (paramārtha) of dharmas (dharma),
it too is suchness (tathatā);
for at every time it is just so—
that very suchness is representation-only-ness (vijñaptimātratā).
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation could render paramārtha as a loose “highest purpose” and miss Sthiramati’s definition as the object of supreme supramundane cognition and as pariniṣpanna-svabhāva.
It could misread tathatāpi as tathā api, “thus also/although,” whereas the bhāṣya treats it as tathatā api and explains apiśabda.
It could miss the etymological force of tathābhāvāt—“because it is always just so, not otherwise”—or even split it wrongly as tathā-abhāvāt.
It could take vijñaptimātratā as something distinct from suchness/perfected nature; Sthiramati explicitly rejects that by glossing sā eva.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5’s parama_Cp + arthaḥ_SNM segmentation is retained only as compound analysis; the verse pada is analyzed as the single subanta paramārthaḥ, with the tatpuruṣa vigraha supplied by trbh 1414.
ByT5 gives no features for yatas; I analyze pausal yataḥ as a causal avyaya because Sthiramati paraphrases it with yasmāt ... tasmāt at trbh 1418–1420.
ByT5 gives sarvakālam without features; I analyze it as an indeclinable adverb, supported by its commentarial use with bhavati at trbh 1432.
ByT5 splits vijñaptimātratā as vijñaptimātra_Cp + tā_SNF; I analyze the printed verse-word as one feminine nominative singular abstract noun, with only an inner vijñapti-mātra compound.
